force production.senior: Maximise peak vertical force into the bed. The knee extensors and plantar flexors are the primary drivers. Trunk work is stiffness — the rigid link that transfers lower-body force without energy leak.
force production.youth: Build maximum pushing power into the trampoline bed. Your quads and calves do the heavy lifting. Trunk work keeps your body stiff so none of that power is wasted.
force production.junior: Get stronger at pushing into the trampoline. Your legs do the work, and your body stays tight so all that strength goes into your jumps.

repeated power.senior: Sustain high power output across multiple jumps. Priority shifts from peak force to rate of force development and elastic energy return. Reactive contacts, shorter rest, and repeated take-off quality are the key targets.
repeated power.youth: Keep your jump power high from the first bounce to the last. The focus is on fast contacts, bouncing back quickly, and not losing quality as you get tired.
repeated power.junior: Stay powerful through every single jump in your routine. Quick feet, fast bouncing, and keeping your technique even when your legs are tired.

injury resilience.senior: Build tissue tolerance in the structures most loaded by trampoline — patellar tendon, Achilles, and soleus. Exercises are slower, controlled, and progressively loaded. Overhead and brace work protects the shoulder and trunk under fatigue.
injury resilience.youth: Strengthen the tendons and muscles that take the most impact in trampoline. Exercises are slower and controlled to build up the body's ability to handle repeated landings and jumps.
injury resilience.junior: Make your body tougher so it can handle all the jumping and landing. These exercises are done slowly and carefully to build strong muscles and joints.

force production.senior: Strength → Jumps → Brace → Overhead. Heavy bilateral loading primes the neuromuscular system via post-activation potentiation (PAP); jumps follow while that window is active. Brace reinforces force transfer under accumulated load. Overhead closes — precision work whilst the legs recover.
force production.youth: Strength → Jumps → Brace → Overhead. Heavy lifts first because they prime your body for explosive work. The jumps come straight after while that effect is still active. Brace and overhead finish the session.
force production.junior: Strength → Jumps → Brace → Overhead. Big lifts wake your legs up. Jumps come next while your legs are fired up. Then we do body control and shoulder work to finish.

repeated power.senior: Jumps → Strength → Brace → Overhead. Reactive contacts and rate of force development require peak nervous system availability — jumps go first. Power-oriented strength consolidates the pattern under load. Brace and overhead close when systemic fatigue is highest.
repeated power.youth: Jumps → Strength → Brace → Overhead. Jumps go first when your nervous system is freshest — that is when you can produce the fastest contacts. Strength work follows to back it up with power under load.
repeated power.junior: Jumps → Strength → Brace → Overhead. Jumping goes first because your body is freshest and can move the fastest. Strength exercises come after to make your legs even stronger.

injury resilience.senior: Overhead → Strength → Brace → Jumps. Shoulder position work requires body awareness and precision — done fresh. Eccentric strength loading follows; it does not need peak neural drive but requires attention. Brace builds positional capacity under fatigue. Controlled reactive work closes the session as tissue exposure, not power output.
injury resilience.youth: Overhead → Strength → Brace → Jumps. Shoulder work goes first because it needs the most concentration and body awareness. Slow strength follows, then brace work. Controlled jumps finish to expose the tissues to light landing work.
injury resilience.junior: Overhead → Strength → Brace → Jumps. Shoulder exercises first while you are concentrating the hardest. Then slow strength work, body control, and gentle landing practice at the end.

coach.jumps: J — JUMPS  (reactive contacts, elastic contribution, take-off quality)
coach.overhead: O — OVERHEAD  (shoulder strength at depression, scapular control)
coach.brace: B — BRACE  (trunk stiffness for force transfer, not trunk movement)
coach.strength: S — STRENGTH  (knee extensors and plantar flexors)

coach.focus: Movement quality over weight; learn or reconfirm every cue
coach.note: This is the reference week — record loads and positions for comparison

athlete.senior: This week establishes your baseline. Focus on nailing every position and cue. Load is moderate — quality of movement is the only standard.
athlete.youth: Starting week. Get every position right and learn the cues. Don't chase weight — focus on doing each exercise perfectly.
athlete.junior: This is the starting week. Learn every exercise properly and focus on doing them really well. Don't worry about going heavy yet.

coach.purpose: Increase load or volume from W1
coach.focus: Quality must match or improve before anything increases
coach.load: ~80% 1RM compound lifts; small progressions on accessories
coach.note: Only progress exercises where W1 positions were solid

athlete.senior: Add load or reps from last week. Only increase if your positions were solid — quality must match or improve before weight goes up.
athlete.youth: Step it up from last week. Add a little more weight or an extra set, but only if last week's technique was good.
athlete.junior: A bit harder than last week. You can add a little weight or do more reps, but only if you were doing the exercises well last week.

coach.purpose: Push intensity — highest demand week of the block
coach.focus: Compete with W2 numbers; intent and position are the metrics
coach.load: ~85% 1RM compound lifts; highest volume on accessories
coach.note: Watch for technical breakdown under fatigue — positions matter most here

athlete.senior: Push intensity this week. Compete with last week's numbers. Lifts should feel heavy and fast. Jump quality is the standard.
athlete.youth: Hardest week. Push yourself on every exercise. Try to beat last week's numbers but keep the technique locked in.
athlete.junior: This is the toughest week. Work really hard but keep doing everything properly. Your technique matters the most.

coach.purpose: Peak load, fewest reps — maximum intent per repetition
coach.focus: Let the quality demonstrate the adaptation
coach.load: 87–90% 1RM on key lifts; reduced volume across all exercises
coach.note: Every rep is a display of the quality built across the previous three weeks

athlete.senior: Peak week. Heaviest loads, fewest reps. Maximum intent on every single rep. Let your movement quality show the work you have done.
athlete.youth: Final week of the block. Heaviest weights but fewer reps. Make every single rep count — this is where you show what you have built.
athlete.junior: Last week of the block. The weights are the heaviest but you do fewer reps. Make every single one your best.

note.senior: Approaching competition — volume reduced, intensity maintained.
note.youth: Competition is getting close — doing less volume to stay fresh.
note.junior: Competition is coming up — we do a bit less so your body stays fresh.

note.senior: Taper phase — reduced volume, no heavy eccentrics, no new exercises.
note.youth: Taper week — lighter sessions to keep you sharp without tiring you out.
note.junior: Easy week before competition — light sessions to keep your body ready.

note.senior: Competition week — activation only. No strength work. Stay sharp.
note.youth: Competition week — just light activation to keep everything switched on.
note.junior: Competition week — just gentle warm-up exercises to stay ready.

description.senior: Ankle stiffness is the primary limiter — biasing jump and calf volume.
description.youth: Your ankle stiffness is the main area to improve — extra calf and jump work this block.
description.junior: We are focusing on making your ankles stronger and stiffer for jumping.

description.senior: Low force output is the primary limiter — biasing knee extensor and plantar flexor volume.
description.youth: Your pushing force needs work — extra strength exercises for your legs this block.
description.junior: We are focusing on making your legs stronger for bigger pushes into the trampoline.

description.senior: Hip stability is the primary limiter — biasing lateral chain and glute med work.
description.youth: Your hip stability needs work — extra side-body and glute exercises this block.
description.junior: We are focusing on making your hips strong and steady for better landings.

description.senior: Trunk stiffness is the primary limiter — biasing anti-extension and force transfer work.
description.youth: Your trunk stiffness needs work — extra bracing and body-tension exercises this block.
description.junior: We are focusing on keeping your body really tight and strong in the middle.

description.senior: Overhead control is the primary limiter — biasing scapular and shoulder position work.
description.youth: Your overhead position needs work — extra shoulder and scapula exercises this block.
description.junior: We are focusing on making your shoulders strong and steady when your arms are up.

cue.senior: Brace as though the bed is about to contact you; drive the floor away, not the bar up; 3 min rest
cue.youth: Brace your trunk hard, then push the floor away from you; 3 min rest between sets
cue.junior: Squeeze your tummy tight, then push the ground away with your feet; rest 3 minutes

cue.senior: One extra set; same brace and drive intent; do not reduce rest time
cue.youth: One more set than last week; same technique, same rest
cue.junior: One more set than last week; do it exactly the same way

cue.senior: Heavier, fewer reps; reset the full brace before each descent, not just at the top
cue.youth: Heavier but fewer reps; reset your brace before every single squat
cue.junior: Heavier but you do fewer; get tight before every single squat

cue.senior: Peak load; 3–4 min rest; treat every rep as a competition attempt
cue.youth: Heaviest week; long rest; make every single rep your best
cue.junior: The heaviest week; lots of rest; make every rep perfect

cue.senior: Hold the dumbbell at chest height; stay upright and drive through the whole foot; brace hard at the bottom
cue.youth: Hold the weight at your chest; stay tall and push the ground away
cue.junior: Hold the weight at your chest; stand up tall and push hard through your feet

cue.senior: One extra set; same upright torso and full brace; increase load only if positions were solid
cue.youth: One more set; keep the same tall position; only go heavier if last week was clean
cue.junior: One more set; stay tall; only use a heavier weight if last week was easy

cue.senior: Fewer reps, heavier; full depth and brace reset at the top of each rep
cue.youth: Fewer reps, heavier weight; full depth and reset your brace each time
cue.junior: Fewer reps but heavier; go all the way down and get tight at the top each time

cue.senior: Peak load; maintain full depth and upright torso; 2 min rest between sets
cue.youth: Heaviest week; keep full depth and stay tall; 2 min rest
cue.junior: Heaviest week; go all the way down and stay tall; rest 2 minutes

cue.senior: Pause at the bottom for a full 3 seconds; maintain trunk brace and upright position throughout
cue.youth: Hold at the bottom for 3 seconds; stay tall and keep your trunk tight
cue.junior: Hold at the bottom and count to 3; stay tall and keep your tummy tight

cue.senior: Extra set; the pause should feel harder, not easier — maintain the brace throughout
cue.youth: One more set; the hold should still feel hard — don't relax at the bottom
cue.junior: One more set; keep squeezing tight at the bottom, don't relax

cue.senior: Longer pause; fewer reps; this builds the same position strength as a loaded squat
cue.youth: Longer hold at the bottom; fewer reps; this builds real strength in that position
cue.junior: Hold at the bottom for 5 seconds this time; fewer reps but harder

cue.senior: Peak time under tension; maintain full brace quality across all five sets
cue.youth: Hardest week; keep the quality across every set; don't rush the holds
cue.junior: Hardest week; keep every hold strong; don't rush

cue.senior: Front shin stays vertical; push through the whole foot; feel the quad loading directly into the knee
cue.youth: Keep your front shin upright; push through your whole foot; feel the front leg doing the work
cue.junior: Keep your front shin straight up; push through your whole foot; feel your front leg working hard

cue.senior: Same shin position; add load only if W1 quality was clean
cue.youth: Same shin position with weights; only add load if last week's form was good
cue.junior: Same position but with weights; only use them if last week was good

cue.senior: Extra set; knee tracks over the 2nd toe throughout the full range
cue.youth: Extra set, heavier; make sure your knee points over your second toe the whole time
cue.junior: One more set with more weight; keep your knee pointing over your toes

cue.senior: Heaviest loading; 2 min rest between legs; this is the unilateral bed drive pattern
cue.youth: Heaviest week; 2 min rest between legs; this is how one leg drives into the bed
cue.junior: Heaviest week; rest 2 minutes between legs; this is one leg pushing hard

strength.name: Dumbbell Split Squat (both feet on floor)

cue.senior: Feet split front and back; lower until the back knee nearly touches the floor; drive up through the front foot
cue.youth: Split your feet; lower until your back knee nearly touches the floor; push up through the front foot
cue.junior: One foot forward, one back; go down until your back knee almost touches the floor; push up with the front leg

cue.senior: Increase load; maintain the knee tracking and upright trunk
cue.youth: Heavier this week; keep your knee tracking and body upright
cue.junior: A bit heavier; keep your body tall and your knee going the right way

cue.youth: Extra set; fewer reps; go all the way down and control it

cue.senior: Peak loading; 90 secs rest between legs; same range and control as W1
cue.youth: Heaviest week; same depth and control as the first week
cue.junior: Heaviest week; go just as low and just as controlled as week 1

weeks.prescription: 3 x 8 each leg with 2-sec pause at bottom

cue.youth: Step back, hold for 2 seconds at the bottom, then push up with the front leg
cue.junior: Step back, hold for 2 seconds, then push up with the front leg

cue.senior: Extra set; maintain the pause quality and upright trunk

cue.junior: One more set; keep holding and stay tall

cue.senior: Peak volume and time under tension; same depth and control across all sets

cue.junior: Hardest week; keep going just as deep and just as controlled

strength.name: Heavy Standing Calf Raise (machine or barbell)

cue.senior: 3-sec descent, explosive drive up; load the bottom stretch, not just the shortened position at the top
cue.youth: 3 seconds going down, fast going up; feel the full stretch at the bottom

cue.senior: Extra set; only progress if W2 reps were explosive from the full bottom range

cue.junior: One more set; only go heavier if last week felt strong

cue.senior: Peak ankle loading; 2 min rest; this is the plantar flexor drive that completes every take-off
cue.youth: Heaviest calf week; 2 min rest; this is the ankle push that finishes every jump
cue.junior: Heaviest week for your calves; rest 2 minutes; this is the push that finishes every jump

cue.junior: One more set; keep going all the way down even with more weight

cue.senior: Extra set; maintain quality across all four sets; this is significant calf volume at bodyweight

cue.junior: A bit heavier; you must still be just as fast or go back to lighter weights

cue.junior: One more set; fewer reps; keep being fast on every set

weeks.prescription: 5 x 4 with 2-sec pause before each jump

cue.junior: One more set; this is the exact shape your body needs on every single jump

cue.senior: Extra set; maintain contact quality across all sets

cue.senior: More reps; this is the elastic contact pattern used at every bed contact

cue.senior: Peak volume; this directly mirrors the sustained ankle demand of a full 10-jump routine

cue.junior: A bit heavier; being fast matters more than the weight

cue.youth: One more set with weights; these are the shoulder positions used at maximum trampoline depression
cue.junior: One more set with weights; these are the shoulder positions your body uses on the trampoline
